questions.text: It's Monday morning. You have four hours of uninterrupted time. Rank these by what would leave you most energised by lunchtime (1 = most energising, 4 = least):

options.text: Writing a campaign narrative that gives people chills
Mapping out how to outmanoeuvre your competition over the next 18 months
Digging into performance data to find the insight everyone else missed
Getting a chaotic project back on track. Aligning the team, sorting the plan, making it run

questions.text: You have 100 points representing your energy budget for the week. Distribute them based on what genuinely fills your tank, not what you're supposed to say:

options.text: Creating content, writing copy, developing brand voice
Thinking about strategy, positioning, and competitive moves
Analysing data, running tests, optimising based on evidence
Coordinating people, managing projects, making sure things actually get delivered

questions.text: You just won an award. Rank these achievements by which would make you most proud (1 = most fulfilling, 4 = least):

options.text: Your campaign moved thousands of people emotionally and went viral
Your strategic insight completely changed the business trajectory
Your analysis uncovered the one insight that made everyone say 'of course'
You rallied a team through an impossible deadline and delivered something brilliant

questions.text: Which type of work makes you think 'ugh, not this again' when it lands on your desk?

options.text: Anything requiring creative writing or conceptual thinking
Big-picture strategic planning without clear next steps
Spreadsheets, data analysis, or number-crunching
Chasing people, coordinating timelines, or keeping everyone aligned on what's happening next

questions.text: If you had to bet on where your raw natural talent sits, regardless of what your CV says, distribute 100 points:

options.text: Brand storytelling and compelling creative
Strategic thinking and market positioning
Data science and performance optimisation
Leading execution. Getting the right people doing the right things at the right time

questions.text: If you could restart your career with guaranteed success in any path, where would you put your 100 points?

options.text: Becoming a sought-after creative director or brand builder
Becoming a trusted strategic advisor to executives
Becoming a world-class marketing scientist and analyst
Becoming the person everyone wants running their biggest, most complex projects

questions.text: A competitor launches something new. What's your FIRST instinct?

options.text: Check out their messaging. How are they telling the story?
Analyse the strategic implications. What market are they going after?
Look for performance signals. Is it actually working?
Think about what it took to pull that off. The team, the coordination, the moving parts

questions.text: You're reviewing any piece of marketing work. You FIRST focus on:

options.text: Does this message land emotionally? Will people actually care?
Is the positioning sharp? How does this differentiate us?
Do we have data supporting these claims and assumptions?
Can we actually deliver this? Do we have the right people and resources to make it happen?

questions.text: In team discussions, you're usually the one who:

options.text: Brings it back to the customer. What will they feel?
Asks the strategic questions. How does this position us?
Asks for the data. What do the numbers actually show?
Keeps everyone focused. Who's doing what, by when, and what do they need?

questions.text: When you think about great marketing, you FIRST think about:

options.text: Whether it created something that emotionally moved people
Whether it achieved a sustainable competitive advantage
Whether the data proves it drove measurable business impact
Whether the team behind it delivered brilliantly under pressure

questions.text: You get MOST frustrated when marketing work:

options.text: Lacks emotional resonance and feels soulless
Has no clear strategic direction or positioning
Isn't grounded in data or measurement
Falls apart because nobody took ownership of making it actually happen

questions.text: Your instinct for solving problems is:

options.text: Find the human story or emotional insight that unlocks it
Understand the strategic dynamics and competitive context
Look for data patterns that reveal the real issue
Work out who needs to do what, remove the blockers, and get things moving

questions.text: When making important marketing decisions, you are MOST influenced by:

options.text: Intuitive sense of what will resonate emotionally with the audience
Strategic analysis of competitive dynamics and market positioning
Data and empirical evidence from testing and measurement
What's realistic given the team, the timeline, and the resources we actually have

questions.text: You feel MOST confident in a decision when:

options.text: You intuitively know it will connect emotionally with people
You've thought through the strategic implications thoroughly
You have data that validates the approach
You know exactly who's delivering what and that the team can actually pull it off

questions.text: When you have free time to think about marketing, you naturally gravitate toward:

options.text: Imagining creative concepts and narrative possibilities
Thinking about strategic positioning and competitive moves
Analysing performance patterns and optimisation opportunities
Figuring out how to make things run better. Smoother handoffs, clearer ownership, fewer bottlenecks

questions.text: When thinking about how to improve marketing, you most naturally focus on:

options.text: Making our narrative and creative more compelling
Sharpening our strategic positioning and differentiation
Optimising performance through better measurement and testing
Getting the team better organised. Clearer roles, better tools, fewer things falling through the cracks

questions.text: You're MOST inspired by marketing examples that show:

options.text: Brilliant creative that made people stop scrolling and actually feel something
Smart positioning that completely changed how a brand was perceived
Data-driven decisions that turned a struggling campaign into a success story
A team that delivered something ambitious on time, on budget, and made it look easy

questions.text: When forced to choose between marketing approaches, you typically prioritise:

options.text: Creative impact and emotional connection over other considerations
Strategic soundness and competitive advantage over other factors
Data-backed proof and measurable results over other elements
Deliverability. Can we actually get this done well with the people and time we have?

questions.text: Think of your single most fulfilling professional moment. It primarily involved:

options.text: Creating something that resonated deeply with an audience
Developing a strategic insight that changed everything
Discovering a key insight in data that proved your hypothesis
Bringing a team together to deliver something everyone said couldn't be done

questions.text: Early in your career, before you specialised into specific roles, you were MOST naturally drawn to:

options.text: Anything involving creativity, writing, designing, or storytelling
Understanding business strategy and competitive dynamics
Working with data, metrics, and analytical problem-solving
Being the one who organised things. The group project leader, the person who made things happen

questions.text: You've experienced 'flow state' (completely absorbed, time flies) MOST often when:

options.text: Creating content, developing narratives, or doing creative work
Thinking through strategic problems and positioning challenges
Analysing data, running tests, or optimising performance
Running a complex project. Keeping all the plates spinning and the team aligned

questions.text: The professional recognition that meant MOST to you was when someone said:

options.text: Your creative work is inspiring and emotionally powerful
Your strategic thinking changed how we compete
Your analytical insights drove our best decisions
We couldn't have pulled this off without you holding it all together

questions.text: Your proudest professional accomplishment was:

options.text: Creating work that emotionally moved thousands of people
Developing a strategic approach that repositioned the business
Proving a hypothesis that drove major business decisions
Delivering a project everyone thought was impossible. On time, with a team that trusted you

questions.text: If money, title, and career progression didn't matter at all, you would spend your days:

options.text: Creating. Writing, designing, developing narratives and brand stories
Strategising. Advising on positioning, competitive strategy, and vision
Analysing. Discovering insights in data and optimising performance
Running things. Leading teams, managing complex projects, being the person who makes it all come together

questions.text: What percentage of your current work time is spent on each type of activity? (Must total 100%):

options.text: Creative, content, and storytelling work (developing narratives, brand, messaging)
Strategic work (positioning, planning, competitive analysis, frameworks)
Analytical work (data analysis, testing, measurement, optimisation)
Execution work (project management, team coordination, stakeholder management, delivery)

questions.text: Which ONE statement best describes your PRIMARY role responsibility:

options.text: Creating compelling content, brand narratives, and customer-facing creative
Developing strategy, positioning, and competitive frameworks
Analysing performance data, testing, and optimisation
Managing delivery. Projects, people, timelines, and making sure things get done

questions.text: Which activity do you do MOST FREQUENTLY in your day-to-day work:

options.text: Creating content, developing creative, writing, designing
Strategic planning, competitive analysis, positioning work
Working with data, analysing metrics, running tests
Coordinating people, managing timelines, chasing deliverables, running meetings

questions.text: Your current role MOST requires expertise in:

options.text: Storytelling, creative development, brand building, communication
Strategic thinking, competitive analysis, positioning, planning
Data analysis, statistical methods, testing, measurement
Project leadership, stakeholder management, team coordination, getting things across the line

questions.text: You MOST frequently interact with:

options.text: Creative teams, agencies, brand and content stakeholders
Executive leadership, strategy teams, competitive intel
Data and analytics teams, performance marketing, growth teams
Everyone. You're the one connecting project teams, stakeholders, and external partners

questions.text: If you read your actual job description, it PRIMARILY emphasises:

options.text: Creative excellence and storytelling capabilities
Strategic thinking and competitive positioning
Analytical rigour and data-driven decision making
Delivery, coordination, and making sure marketing actually ships

sectionBreaks.6:
You're doing great. So far we're looking at what energises you versus what drains you.

That Storyteller whose brain was fried by 4pm because they'd spent all day fixing automations? Or the Builder who blitzed through the operational work but felt completely wasted?

We're mapping your version of that.
sectionBreaks.12:
Halfway there. Now we're looking at your instincts. The things you do before you even think about it.

These aren't learned behaviours. They're hardwired.

The Strategist who can't help but see competitive implications in everything. The Scientist who instinctively asks 'but what does the data say?' before opinions.

What's your automatic response?
sectionBreaks.18:
Nearly there. This last section is the most important.

We're going to access the version of you that existed before years of role conditioning reshaped how you work.

Before you learned to say you loved data when you didn't. Before you stopped admitting you found operational work draining. Before the job changed you.

Who were you then?
sectionBreaks.24:
Now for the uncomfortable bit.

The next 6 questions are about your actual working life. Not what you wish it was, but what it is.

Be honest, even if it's depressing. The bigger the gap between this section and the last one, the more we can help.
